From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

New Zealand children's rights researcher

| Anne Briar Smith  [CNZM](/wiki/Companion_of_the_New_Zealand_Order_of_Merit "Companion of the New Zealand Order of Merit") [FRSNZ](/wiki/Fellow_of_the_Royal_Society_of_New_Zealand "Fellow of the Royal Society of New Zealand") | |
| --- | --- |
| Born | Anne Briar Riddall  (1940-08-13)13 August 1940  [Porthcawl](/wiki/Porthcawl "Porthcawl"), Wales |
| Died | 22 May 2016(2016-05-22) (aged 75)  [Dunedin](/wiki/Dunedin "Dunedin"), New Zealand |
| Spouse | John Smith |
|  | |
| Academic background | |
| [Alma mater](/wiki/Alma_mater "Alma mater") | [University of Alberta](/wiki/University_of_Alberta "University of Alberta") |
| [Thesis](/wiki/Thesis "Thesis") | * *Verbalization and selective attention in discrimination shift problems* (1971) |
| Academic work | |
| Discipline | * Children's rights * violence against children * gender stereotyping |
| Institutions | [University of Otago](/wiki/University_of_Otago "University of Otago") |
| Doctoral students | [Nicola Taylor](/wiki/Nicola_Taylor "Nicola Taylor") |
|  | |

**Anne Briar Smith** [CNZM](/wiki/Companion_of_the_New_Zealand_Order_of_Merit "Companion of the New Zealand Order of Merit") [FRSNZ](/wiki/Fellow_of_the_Royal_Society_of_New_Zealand "Fellow of the Royal Society of New Zealand") (née **Riddall**, 13 August 1940 – 22 May 2016) was a New Zealand professor at the [University of Otago](/wiki/University_of_Otago "University of Otago"), and was a pioneering children's rights researcher.

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anne_Smith_(academic)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Smith was born on 13 August 1940 in [Porthcawl](/wiki/Porthcawl "Porthcawl"), Wales, to Dora and Geoff Riddall. Her father worked for the [Iraq Petroleum Company](/wiki/Iraq_Petroleum_Company "Iraq Petroleum Company"), and when that necessitated the family move to Syria, Smith boarded at the [Welsh Girls' School](/wiki/Welsh_Girls%E2%80%99_School "Welsh Girls’ School") in England, aged nine.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) The family moved to New Zealand in 1954, and Smith attended [Te Aroha College](/wiki/Te_Aroha_College "Te Aroha College"), where she was dux in both 1956 and 1957.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)

## Academic career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anne_Smith_(academic)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Academic career")]

Smith completed a BHSc in Home Science at the University of Otago in 1963, and then completed a BA in Education at both Otago and [Auckland Universities](/wiki/University_of_Auckland "University of Auckland") in 1965.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) She then undertook an MEd at the [University of Alberta](/wiki/University_of_Alberta "University of Alberta") in 1969, and then completed a [PhD](/wiki/PhD "PhD") titled *Verbalization and selective attention in discrimination shift problems* there in 1971. She was supported during her PhD by a [Commonwealth Scholarship](/wiki/Commonwealth_Scholarship_and_Fellowship_Plan "Commonwealth Scholarship and Fellowship Plan") and an [Izaack Walton Killam](/wiki/Izaak_Walton_Killam "Izaak Walton Killam") Memorial Fellowship.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) After Smith finished her PhD, she and her husband spent two years working for an experimental tertiary education institution in Halifax, Nova Scotia.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)

In 1974, Smith was appointed as a lecturer in education and human development at the University of Otago.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) By this time she had two young girls, making her research on quality early childhood education especially relevant. She was instrumental in getting the [Dunedin Community Childcare Association](/w/index.php?title=Dunedin_Community_Childcare_Association&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dunedin Community Childcare Association (page does not exist)") established.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) She also made four films, including *Blue for a Girl*, about gender stereotyping.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)

Smith became the inaugural director of the Children's Issues Centre at the University of Otago in 1995.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2) Her work focused in ways to improve children's lives, especially in times of stress such as when parents divorce. She worked to ban physical punishment of children.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) Notable doctoral students of Smith's include [Nicola Taylor](/wiki/Nicola_Taylor "Nicola Taylor"), who succeeded her as director of the Centre.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Her book *Understanding Children's Development* was first published in 1982, became a standard text, and is in its fifth edition (now titled *Understanding children and childhood*).[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)

## Family

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anne_Smith_(academic)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Family")]

Smith married Dr John Smith in Edmonton, Canada, in May 1967.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) They had two daughters.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) Smith died on 22 May 2016 in Dunedin.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)

## Honours and legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anne_Smith_(academic)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Honours and legacy")]

Smith was elected a Fellow of the [Royal Society Te Apārangi](/wiki/Royal_Society_Te_Ap%C4%81rangi "Royal Society Te Apārangi") in 1995, and appointed a [Companion of the New Zealand Order of Merit](/wiki/Companion_of_the_New_Zealand_Order_of_Merit "Companion of the New Zealand Order of Merit"), for services to children, in the [2007 New Year Honours](/wiki/2007_New_Year_Honours_(New_Zealand) "2007 New Year Honours (New Zealand)").[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2) Smith was awarded an honorary doctorate by the [University of Oulu](/wiki/University_of_Oulu "University of Oulu") in Finland in 1998.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2) In 2013 Smith was awarded a New Zealand–UK Link Foundation visiting professorship, enabling her to give a series of public lectures in London on children’s rights and the links between government policy and research on children’s issues. On the same trip, she spoke to a [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") select committee and the [Welsh Assembly](/wiki/Senedd_Cymru_%E2%80%93_Welsh_Parliament "Senedd Cymru – Welsh Parliament") against physical violence towards children.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)

After Smith's death, her colleagues were inspired to write a book honouring her achievements, entitled *Research, policy and advocacy in the early years: Writing inspired by the achievements of Professor Anne Smith*, edited by [Carmen Dalli](/wiki/Carmen_Dalli "Carmen Dalli") and Anne Meade. Smith was selected as one of the Royal Society Te Apārangi's [150 women in 150 words](/wiki/150_women_in_150_words "150 women in 150 words") in 2017.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)
